concept_records.canonical_key: decoupling of logical validity and semantic plausibility decoupling of logical validity and semantic plausibility a faithful assessment of logical reasoning requires test instances where the truth value of the conclusion derived from premises is orthogonal to its semantic plausibility in the real wo
concept_records.canonical_label: Decoupling of Logical Validity and Semantic Plausibility

payload.abstract_signature: A faithful assessment of logical reasoning requires test instances where the truth value of the conclusion derived from premises is orthogonal to its semantic plausibility in the real world.
payload.argument: A faithful assessment of logical reasoning requires test instances where the truth value of the conclusion derived from premises is orthogonal to its semantic plausibility in the real world.
payload.canonical_key: faithful assessment of logical reasoning requires test instances where truth value of conclusion derived from premises is orthogonal to its semantic plausibility in real world
payload.discussion: The principle dictates that any framework aiming to extract or evaluate logical patterns must actively engineer conflicts between 'what makes sense' and 'what follows logically.' If a dataset allows a model to succeed by ignoring logic, it fails as a logical reasoning benchmark. The application extends to training data generation: improving reasoning frontiers requires exposing models to examples where following the logical pattern leads to surprising or non-obvious outcomes, thereby strengthening the model's ability to override semantic priors with syntactic rules.
payload.evidence: The work demonstrates that existing benchmarks like ReClor and LogiQA allow models to perform better when logical reasoning capabilities are explicitly suppressed via prompting, indicating that these benchmarks measure commonsense alignment rather than logical rigor. In contrast, the proposed DivLogicEval constructs instances specifically to break this correlation, using a logic solver to validate that the only path to the correct answer is through formal derivation, regardless of how counterintuitive the result appears.
payload.name: Decoupling of Logical Validity and Semantic Plausibility
payload.problem_pressure: I would like to design a framework that lets LLMs extract logical patterns from other exemplars during inference and use the logical patterns to improve the reasoning accuracy-token frontier.
payload.source_paper_title: DivLogicEval: A Framework for Benchmarking Logical Reasoning Evaluation in Large Language Models
payload.source_work_title: DivLogicEval: A Framework for Benchmarking Logical Reasoning Evaluation in Large Language Models

payload.abstract_signature: A faithful assessment of logical reasoning requires test instances where the truth value of the conclusion derived from premises is orthogonal to its semantic plausibility in the real world.
payload.argument: A faithful assessment of logical reasoning requires test instances where the truth value of the conclusion derived from premises is orthogonal to its semantic plausibility in the real world.
payload.discussion: The principle dictates that any framework aiming to extract or evaluate logical patterns must actively engineer conflicts between 'what makes sense' and 'what follows logically.' If a dataset allows a model to succeed by ignoring logic, it fails as a logical reasoning benchmark. The application extends to training data generation: improving reasoning frontiers requires exposing models to examples where following the logical pattern leads to surprising or non-obvious outcomes, thereby strengthening the model's ability to override semantic priors with syntactic rules.
payload.evidence: The work demonstrates that existing benchmarks like ReClor and LogiQA allow models to perform better when logical reasoning capabilities are explicitly suppressed via prompting, indicating that these benchmarks measure commonsense alignment rather than logical rigor. In contrast, the proposed DivLogicEval constructs instances specifically to break this correlation, using a logic solver to validate that the only path to the correct answer is through formal derivation, regardless of how counterintuitive the result appears.
payload.name: Decoupling of Logical Validity and Semantic Plausibility
payload.problem_pressure: I would like to design a framework that lets LLMs extract logical patterns from other exemplars during inference and use the logical patterns to improve the reasoning accuracy-token frontier.
payload.source_paper_title: DivLogicEval: A Framework for Benchmarking Logical Reasoning Evaluation in Large Language Models
payload.source_work_title: DivLogicEval: A Framework for Benchmarking Logical Reasoning Evaluation in Large Language Models

payload.abstract_signature: A faithful assessment of logical reasoning requires test instances where the truth value of the conclusion derived from premises is orthogonal to its semantic plausibility in the real world.
payload.argument: A faithful assessment of logical reasoning requires test instances where the truth value of the conclusion derived from premises is orthogonal to its semantic plausibility in the real world.

payload.discussion: The principle dictates that any framework aiming to extract or evaluate logical patterns must actively engineer conflicts between 'what makes sense' and 'what follows logically.' If a dataset allows a model to succeed by ignoring logic, it fails as a logical reasoning benchmark. The application extends to training data generation: improving reasoning frontiers requires exposing models to examples where following the logical pattern leads to surprising or non-obvious outcomes, thereby strengthening the model's ability to override semantic priors with syntactic rules.
payload.evidence: The work demonstrates that existing benchmarks like ReClor and LogiQA allow models to perform better when logical reasoning capabilities are explicitly suppressed via prompting, indicating that these benchmarks measure commonsense alignment rather than logical rigor. In contrast, the proposed DivLogicEval constructs instances specifically to break this correlation, using a logic solver to validate that the only path to the correct answer is through formal derivation, regardless of how counterintuitive the result appears.

concept_records.canonical_key: performance degradation under logic suppression reveals benchmark flaws performance degradation under logic suppression reveals benchmark flaws large language models often achieve higher or comparable performance on popular logical reasoning benchmarks even when prompted to ignore logical reasoning
concept_records.canonical_label: Performance Degradation Under Logic-Suppression Reveals Benchmark Flaws

payload.canonical_key: large language models often achieve higher or comparable performance on popular logical reasoning benchmarks even when prompted to ignore logical reasoning indicating benchmarks pr
payload.condition: This phenomenon occurs in benchmarks derived from real-world contexts such as LSAT or GMAT questions, where questions inherently contain strong semantic cues and commonsense associations that allow models to bypass formal logic.
payload.discussion: This finding serves as a critical diagnostic for dataset quality: if disabling logic does not significantly drop performance, the dataset is not measuring logic. For designing a framework to extract logical patterns, this implies that relying on existing high-profile datasets may lead to learning spurious correlations rather than genuine logical structures. The lesson is to prioritize synthetic or carefully curated data where semantic shortcuts are explicitly blocked.
payload.evidence: The text notes that by simply reducing the model's ability to utilize logical reasoning through prompting, LLMs surprisingly yield better performance on ReClor and LogiQA, demonstrating that these benchmarks entangle multiple reasoning skills.
payload.finding: Large Language Models often achieve higher or comparable performance on popular logical reasoning benchmarks even when prompted to ignore logical reasoning, indicating these benchmarks primarily test commonsense knowledge rather than logical deduction.
payload.main_results: Large Language Models often achieve higher or comparable performance on popular logical reasoning benchmarks even when prompted to ignore logical reasoning, indicating these benchmarks primarily test commonsense knowledge rather than logical deduction.
payload.message_text: Large Language Models often achieve higher or comparable performance on popular logical reasoning benchmarks even when prompted to ignore logical reasoning, indicating these benchmarks primarily test commonsense knowledge rather than logical deduction.
payload.source_paper_title: DivLogicEval: A Framework for Benchmarking Logical Reasoning Evaluation in Large Language Models
payload.source_work_title: DivLogicEval: A Framework for Benchmarking Logical Reasoning Evaluation in Large Language Models

upload_decisions.title: DivLogicEval: A Framework for Benchmarking Logical Reasoning Evaluation in Large Language Models

identity.canonical_title: DivLogicEval: A Framework for Benchmarking Logical Reasoning Evaluation in Large Language Models
identity.title_norm: divlogiceval a framework for benchmarking logical reasoning evaluation in large language models

work_version_records.abstract: Logic reasoning in natural language has been recognized as an important measure of human intelligence for Large Language Models (LLMs). Popular benchmarks may entangle multiple reasoning skills and thus provide unfaithful evaluations on the logic reasoning skill. Meanwhile, existing logic reasoning benchmarks are limited in language diversity and their distributions are deviated from the distribution of an ideal logic reasoning benchmark, which may lead to biased evaluation results. This paper thereby proposes a new classical logic benchmark DivLogicEval, consisting of natural sentences composed of diverse statements in a counterintuitive way. To ensure a more reliable evaluation, we also introduce a new evaluation metric that mitigates the influence of bias and randomness inherent in LLMs. Through experiments, we demonstrate the extent to which logical reasoning is required to answer the questions in DivLogicEval and compare the performance of different popular LLMs in conducting logical reasoning.

This paper thereby proposes a new classical logic benchmark DivLogicEval, consisting of natural sentences composed of diverse statements in a counterintuitive way. To ensure a more reliable evaluation, we also introduce a new evaluation metric that mitigates the influence of bias and randomness inherent in LLMs. Through experiments, we demonstrate the extent to which logical reasoning is required to answer the questions in DivLogicEval and compare the performance of different popular LLMs in conducting logical reasoning.
